item.title: Middle East conflict impact unclear, says economist
item.description: THE impact of the Middle East conflict which began on Saturday on the oil industry, global trade and markets, and the market in Papua New Guinea (PNG) remains unclear, says an economist. Institute of National Affairs executive director Paul Barker said the impact on global markets largely depended on how protracted the conflict was.
item.content:encoded:
THE impact of the Middle East conflict which began on Saturday on the oil industry, global trade and markets, and the market in Papua New Guinea (PNG) remains unclear, says an economist.

Institute of National Affairs executive director Paul Barker said the impact on global markets largely depended on how protracted the conflict was.

“In PNG, although we have extensive gas, and more modest quantities of oil and condensate, we’re heavily dependent on internationally supplied oil and subject to global prices,” he said.

“Short-term higher crude prices don’t affect prices at the pump immediately. But if disruption is sustained and global prices are driven up, potentially even back over the US$100 (about K436.72) per barrel mark, clearly this will be passed on in wholesale and pump prices in PNG as stocks are progressively replaced.

“Of course, as with earlier rounds of higher energy prices, (such as after the Ukraine invasion) there would be winners and losers. Most businesses and households that are partly dependent upon transported goods and services would see costs rise.

“But clearly energy supplying companies, (including SOEs like Kumul Petroleum) would benefit from higher prices, as would the State both in due course from higher taxes and duties on oil and gas companies and the trade.”

He said the narrow Hormuz Strait through which shipping must travel to access the oil rich ports and refineries of the Gulf Sea handled about a fifth of the global oil trade.

“Currently it’s effectively closed following attacks and sinking of some vessels,” he said.

“The market can readily handle the effective closure of the Strait for a few days. However, if closure was more extended and ports and refineries in the Gulf states substantially damaged, then clearly it would disrupt supplies badly, especially for some consuming countries that are dependent upon production from the Middle Eastern states.

“We’ve already seen the market price jump by 10 per cent at the opening of this week’s trading markets, but then falling back to be about four per cent higher as things settled somewhat.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Fire service conduct hydrant inspection
item.description: THE Papua New Guinea Fire Service conducted a fire hydrant inspection in areas of Lae to make sure they are working and can supply water in the case of a fire. The inspections focused on commercial and high-traffic areas, including Top Town and China Town.
item.content:encoded:
THE Papua New Guinea Fire Service conducted a fire hydrant inspection in areas of Lae to make sure they are working and can supply water in the case of a fire.

The inspections focused on commercial and high-traffic areas, including Top Town and China Town.

A statement from the fire service said: “We check hydrants to make sure they are ready to use, have enough water pressure, and work properly during emergencies.”

In an official statement, the Fire Service said, “Hydrant inspections are conducted to ensure immediate operational readiness, sufficient water pressure is supplied, and proper mechanical functions during emergencies”.

Lae is served by one fire station, covering the entire city.

Authorities have confirmed that a second fire station is under construction at the University of Technology (Unitech) campus.

It will provide fire coverage to the university, East Taraka, and nearby areas.

Fire officers emphasised the importance of community cooperation, urging residents and business owners to keep fire hydrants accessible and to report any damaged units to the nearest station.

Officials said that maintaining operational hydrants was critical to reducing response times and preventing property loss or injury during fires.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Include PNG in evacuation plans, Marape requests
item.description: PRIME Minister James Marape has requested the United Arab Emirates (UAE) and the Australian government to include Papua New Guineans into their evacuation plan when required. This follows the escalating conflict between Iran, Israel and the United States (US).
item.content:encoded:
PRIME Minister James Marape has requested the United Arab Emirates (UAE) and the Australian government to include Papua New Guineans into their evacuation plan when required.

This follows the escalating conflict between Iran, Israel and the United States (US).

As reported in The National yesterday, Israel and the US launched strikes on selected targets in Iran, killing Iran’s Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and immediate family members.

Marape said due to the absence of a PNG diplomatic presence in the UAE, the Government was requesting foreign consulate assistance from both governments to include PNG diaspora and citizens in their contingency and evacuation plans, if the situation warranted emergency repatriation.

“There is no diplomatic presence in the UAE as yet,” he said.

“Alternatively, the Government will seek assistance from the Australian government through the Resident Australian Embassy in Abu Dhabi to include our citizens in their contingency and evacuation plans.”

He added that “our prayers and thoughts are with our PNG citizens and diaspora for their safety”.

“It is my Government’s hope and desire that an amicable truce is reached between all parties concerned,” he said.

“This is necessary for the total restoration of regional and global peace and security in the Middle East and beyond.”

He will write to UAE President Mohammed Bin Zayed Al Nahyan to seek his support for their well-being as and when the need arises.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Court to rule on governorship row
item.description: THE National Court is expected to make a ruling on Friday on the governorship of New Ireland – while restraining provincial seat MP Byron Chan and Namatanai MP Walter Schnaubelt from occupying that office. Judge Pauline Bre yesterday in Waigani restrained Chan and Schnaubelt from performing and excising the powers and functions as New Ireland governor until further orders of the court.
item.content:encoded:
THE National Court is expected to make a ruling on Friday on the governorship of New Ireland – while restraining provincial seat MP Byron Chan and Namatanai MP Walter Schnaubelt from occupying that office.

Judge Pauline Bre yesterday in Waigani restrained Chan and Schnaubelt from performing and excising the powers and functions as New Ireland governor until further orders of the court. 

Chan’s lawyer Alfred Manase had sought a court declaration that Chan was the governor of New Ireland after winning the recent by-election for that seat left vacant by his late father Sir Julius Chan.

Manase submitted that Section 101 of the Constitution clarifies that no MP could represent two or more electorates at the same time.

He added that Section 104 (g) explains that the seat of a Member of the Parliament becomes vacant on his death.

Section 106 provides for a by-election to fill the vacancy.

He argued that Chan was the governor because of Section 17(2) of the Organic Law on Provincial Government and Local Level Government (OLPGLLG).

That issue was determined in Launa v Willie OS 402 of 2004, and Wai v Ambane OS501 of 1998.

Manase said Section 19 dismissed Schnaubelt, who was elected by the provincial assembly, from office as there was no longer a vacancy existing following Chan’s by-election victory.

Schnaubelt’s lawyer Rimbink Pato said there were separate offices under the law, and the matter before the court was for the provincial assembly to decide on.

He said that in Mango v Muthuvel 2019 N7916, whether it was by death or whenever a vacancy arose, the person appointed by the provincial assembly as governor should remain in office.

Judge Bre told the parties that she needed time to go through the submissions before making a ruling.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Cream of the crop
item.description: TWO Papua New Guinea NRL Bid Academy talents have been drafted into this year’s SP PNG Hunters development squad. The recruitment of Hercules Kondo, from the Goroka Lahanis, and David Issac from the Mt Hagen Eagles have received praises far and wide.
item.content:encoded:
TWO Papua New Guinea NRL Bid Academy talents have been drafted into this year’s SP PNG Hunters development squad.

The recruitment of Hercules Kondo, from the Goroka Lahanis, and David Issac from the Mt Hagen Eagles have received praises far and wide.

This week’s notable commendation came from PNG Rugby Football League (PNGRFL) head of elite pathways Joey Grima.

Grima said their enlistment in the PNG Hunters development programme are “rewards for their hard work”.

“I am incredibly proud of David Issac and Hercules Kondo and what they represent for our game in Papua New Guinea,” Grima told The National.

Both players were first identified through a PNGRFL-affiliated schools’ competition.

“They were then selected into the PNG NRL Bid Academy, where they stood out through discipline, strong work ethic and belief in themselves,” Grima said.

Both players were selected in the inaugural National Youth Competition (NYC) competition and, in a special moment for PNG rugby league, they faced each other in last year’s grand final.

Since then, Kondo and Issac have proudly worn the Junior Kumuls jersey.

Now, they have taken another big step forward with their official selection into the Hunters setup.

Under the guidance of coach Paul Aiton in this year’s Hostplus Cup competition, the pair are expected to continue improving their skills and maturing as players.

With excitement growing around Papua New Guinea’s planned entry into the NRL as the Chiefs, Grima said the timing of their success could not be better.

“The system works,” he said. “David and Hercules are living proof that our pathways provide a real platform for young men and women across Papua New Guinea to dream big and achieve their goals.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: New coach promises fresh era for Lahanis
item.description: NEW Bintagor Goroka Lahanis head coach Michael Moses has made it clear that the next chapter for the Lahanis in the Digicel ExxonMobil Cup will be built on transparency, accountability and fairness. He said these values are needed to restore pride in one of the country’s pioneer rugby league franchise.
item.content:encoded:
NEW Bintagor Goroka Lahanis head coach Michael Moses has made it clear that the next chapter for the Lahanis in the Digicel ExxonMobil Cup will be built on transparency, accountability and fairness.

He said these values are needed to restore pride in one of the country’s pioneer rugby league franchise.

“For six years we have been making up the numbers in the competition, even though we had many talented players,” Moses told The National.

“Now it will be different. I will ensure transparency, accountability and fairness in this team.

“Eastern Highlands is bigger than any of us; the Lahanis flag must again fly high.”

Moses also linked the club’s future to Papua New Guinea’s planned entry into the National Rugby League in 2028 as the PNG Chiefs.

“We are entering the NRL in 2028 as the PNG Chiefs. My focus this year will be on pathway development and winning the Cup,” he said.

Moses believes the Lahanis could play a key role in preparing players for the national franchise and eveloping young talent and building a winning culture will be his top priority.

“This team is bigger than any one of us. It represents our culture, heritage and our community as a whole. I want the team to serve its purpose and make Eastern Highlands proud.”

The reference to exporting players highlights Moses’ ambition to see Lahanis talent move on to bigger competitions, including opportunities similar to those enjoyed by the PNG Hunters in the Hostplus Cup.

The coach admitted the road ahead will not be easy. Strong competition, internal rebuilding and high expectations will all test the team this season.

“There will be obstacles and challenges along the way, but by the grace of God we will not back down,” he added.

“We will outsmart and outrun whoever tries to take us down (this season).”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Weather halts PNG-Fiji clash, American Samoa into semis
item.description: THE Fifa Women’s World Cup 2027 Oceania Qualifiers match between PNG Bilums and Fiji was called off yesterday due to adverse weather conditions in Ba, Fiji. Details regarding the rescheduling will be announced later.
item.content:encoded:
THE Fifa Women’s World Cup 2027 Oceania Qualifiers match between PNG Bilums and Fiji was called off yesterday due to adverse weather conditions in Ba, Fiji.

Details regarding the rescheduling will be announced later.

Meanwhile, American Samoa made it two wins from two and booked their place in the semi-finals of the Fifa Women’s World Cup 2027 Oceania Qualifiers with a narrow 1-0 win over Samoa in Honiara.

Having stunned the Solomon Islands on Friday, Amanda Cromwell’s side knew three more points would virtually secure progression to Round 3, and they delivered.

Mia Toeaina came close early with a shot tipped over by Angelique Tuisamoa, while Alayna Fuamatu-Maafala struck the post with a header.

The breakthrough arrived on the stroke of half-time when Toeaina rounded the goalkeeper and squared for Morgan Patea to finish from close range.

Samoa improved after the break, dominating possession in American Samoa’s half, but failed to convert pressure into goals, leaving their qualification hopes hanging by a thread.

Elsewhere, New Caledonia revived their campaign with a 2-1 victory over Vanuatu at a rain-soaked Govind Park in Ba, Fiji, yesterday.

Both sides needed a win after heavy opening defeats, and New Caledonia struck twice in the first half.

Kané Dralu opened the scoring with a superb solo effort in the 14th minute, before Leana Hmae doubled the lead with a header from a corner in the 34th.

Vanuatu, still chasing their first goal of the qualifiers, saw Nettie Kalsau denied by an offside flag and the crossbar.

Leimata Simon finally pulled one back in the 82nd minute, but it proved too late as New Caledonia held firm for their first Fifa Women’s World Cup qualifying win since 2018.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Ex-Muruk credits pioneers
item.description: THE Digicel ExxonMobil Cup, Papua New Guinea’s premier semi-professional rugby league competition, traces its roots back to 1990 when visionary administrators set out to shape the future of the game, a former PRK Mendi Muruks player and assistant coach has said. Thomas Undupie named the pioneers of the semi-professional competition as former Mendi Rugby League and PNGRFL president Joe Keviame (dec
item.content:encoded:
THE Digicel ExxonMobil Cup, Papua New Guinea’s premier semi-professional rugby league competition, traces its roots back to 1990 when visionary administrators set out to shape the future of the game, a former PRK Mendi Muruks player and assistant coach has said.

Thomas Undupie named the pioneers of the semi-professional competition as former Mendi Rugby League and PNGRFL president Joe Keviame (deceased), Kevin Murphy (deceased), John Numapo, and others “who believed in building a pathway for rural talent to shine on the national stage”.

“Their dream was simple yet ambitious: create a competition that would expose raw talent from across PNG and provide opportunities for players to progress into Queensland leagues and, eventually, the NRL.

“More than three decades later, that vision remains central to PNG’s rugby league identity,” Undupie said.

He said while some argue that PNG should now focus on the NRL Bid Academy in preparation for the PNG Chiefs’ entry into the NRL in 2028, “seasoned rugby league veterans insist the Digicel ExxonMobil Cup must remain the heart of the game”.

He warned that the Chiefs are likely to recruit overseas talent, leaving local players sidelined unless the domestic competition continues to thrive. Undupie said despite its longevity, the DEM Cup competition faces challenges.

“One key policy requires franchises to recruit 75 per cent of their players from the host province and the remainder from elsewhere. Yet, compliance has been inconsistent, with sponsors and owners often prioritising results over provincial representation.”

He said honouring this policy is vital to maintaining community identity and grassroots development. Commenting on the Mendi Muruks, Undupie said backed by Petroleum Resources Kutubu (PRK), they stand as one of the competition’s most enduring franchises.

“PRK has supported the Muruks for 13 years, but despite strong backing, the team has struggled to convert investment into consistent premiership success.

“After breaking a 13-year drought to reach last year’s grand final, familiar weaknesses resurfaced, raising questions about management and player development.”

Undupie called on the PRK board to take a more hands-on role in ensuring the Muruks not only chase titles but also produce representative players year after year.

“The Muruks’ future lies in harnessing talent from the Southern Highlands,” he said, adding that last year, eight affiliated leagues were officially recognised under the PNGRFL: And they include Mendi Rugby League; Lai Valley Rugby League; Nipa Rugby League; Aiya Rugby League; Kagua Rugby League; Erave Rugby League; United Imbongu Rugby League; and Pangia Rugby League.

He said it was likely that Sembiringi and Ialibu would join this year.

The Mendi Muruks trial matches for this year would be held in Lae, Morobe.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: TB spread threatening Highlands
item.description: TUBERCULOSIS (TB) has long been a public health challenge in Papua New Guinea. Roughly 5-10 years ago, the disease was most commonly reported in coastal provinces such as Western, Gulf, and Central.
item.content:encoded:
TUBERCULOSIS (TB) has long been a public health challenge in Papua New Guinea.

Roughly 5-10 years ago, the disease was most commonly reported in coastal provinces such as Western, Gulf, and Central.

These areas, with difficult terrains and limited access to health services, became hotspots for transmission.

TB is now spreading rapidly in the Highlands, with Enga standing out as a province where all districts are affected.

This shift highlights how TB is not confined to one region – it adapts to social and environmental conditions, moving where health systems are weakest and communities most vulnerable.

Since, we are prone and vulnerable to sick TB, it is spreading.

The spread of TB in Enga and other Highlands provinces underscores the difficulties faced by communities in both urban and remote locations:

The Government has a crucial role to play in strengthening the TB response.

International donor agencies have been instrumental in funding TB, HIV/AIDS, and other essential medications in PNG.

Their continued support is vital because:

From a layman’s perspective, the message is simple but urgent: TB is spreading in Enga and across the Highlands, and people need help.

The Government must act quickly, and donor agencies must continue their support.

Without sustained intervention, TB will not only persist, but may become more resistant and harder to treat.

TB in PNG is not just a medical issue – it is a social and economic challenge that affects families, communities, and the nation’s future.

The spread into the Highlands, particularly Enga, is a warning sign.

Health workers, government authorities, and donor agencies must work together to ensure that every patient receives timely diagnosis, continuous treatment, and proper education.

Only then can PNG hope to control and eventually eliminate TB.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Make capital city safe and clean
item.description: THE people and community leaders of Port Moresby should give their full support to the recent call by Governor Powes Parkop to reintroduce the Vagrancy Act. Our capital city must not drift further into disorder.
item.content:encoded:
THE people and community leaders of Port Moresby should give their full support to the recent call by Governor Powes Parkop to reintroduce the Vagrancy Act.

Our capital city must not drift further into disorder.

Public harassment, petty crime, public drunkenness, and intimidation in public places affect families, businesses, churches, and visitors.

A capital city must reflect national pride, not fear.

At the same time, enforcement must be fair, lawful, and balanced.

The purpose of the law should not be to punish poverty.

It should be to address behaviour that threatens public safety and peace.

Clear definitions must guide enforcement so that citizens are protected from arbitrary action.

I suggest a practical, future-focused solution to city hall, parliament, government departments, and civil society.

The Royal Papua New Guinea Constabulary must be properly trained, equipped, and supervised.

Independent oversight mechanisms should monitor implementation to prevent abuse or selective targeting.

Second, create real social support systems.

Temporary shelters, counselling services, rehabilitation for drug and alcohol abuse, and job skills training must operate alongside enforcement.

Churches, non-governmental organisations, and the private sector can play a key role in reintegration programmes.

Many street-related issues involve unemployed young people.

Skills training, small business grants, apprenticeships, and structured community work programmes can offer alternatives to street life.

Public education campaigns in schools, settlements, and workplaces should encourage cleanliness, respect for public property, and shared responsibility for maintaining order.

Our end goal is clear: a peaceful, clean, safe, and cooperative capital city – one that can proudly stand among the best cities in the Pacific region.

Order must walk together with compassion.

Strong leadership must walk together with community support.

If we unite around balanced solutions, Port Moresby can become a model capital built on dignity, discipline, and hope.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Prosecution for assault on journalist
item.description: THE Correctional Services (CS) officers (law enforcement officials) who allegedly assaulted the female reporter from The National must face the full brunt of the law. Where in their animalistic brain cells did they decided that journalist were public enemy number one.
item.content:encoded:
THE Correctional Services (CS) officers (law enforcement officials) who allegedly assaulted the female reporter from The National must face the full brunt of the law.

Where in their animalistic brain cells did they decided that journalist were public enemy number one.

The CS personnel thought that allegedly assaulting a journalist the other day was fine.

Well they have something coming for them.

These culprits must be prosecuted, judged and sentenced back to Bomana where they rightfully belong. There is no ifs and no buts.

The journalists and the press are the voice of the people in our communities – they have always been.

As they say hang them high – hang them low.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Gulf must unite to share benefits
item.description: I CALL upon our Gulf people to unite and work hard together to make this great Kerema city dream a reality. The opportunity before us through the Papua liquefied natural gas (LNG) project is rare and important.
item.content:encoded:
I CALL upon our Gulf people to unite and work hard together to make this great Kerema city dream a reality.

The opportunity before us through the Papua liquefied natural gas (LNG) project is rare and important.

If we stand united, we can change the future of our province.

Kerema has the potential to grow into a strong seaport city that serves not only the LNG project, but also fisheries, agriculture, and small local businesses.

But development will not happen by words alone.

It will require discipline, cooperation, and commitment from leaders, landowners, youths, women, churches, and public servants.

We must put aside tribal differences and personal interests.

Land issues must be settled peacefully and fairly.

Leaders must negotiate wisely and ensure benefits are shared across the province.

Funds must be used for hospitals, schools, roads, water supply, housing, and reliable power.

Transparency and accountability are essential so that every Kina is used for the public good.

Young people must prepare themselves through education and skills training so they are ready for jobs that come with development.

Local businesses must also be encouraged to grow and participate in contracts and services opportunities.

Gulf has long been called a sleeping giant.

Let us now rise together with one heart and one purpose to build a clean, organised, and prosperous Kerema for our children and future generations.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Urban elites, rural exclusion and politics of growth
item.description: Neglect of the rural population is frequently attributed to globalisation. But treating globalisation as the sole culprit averts attention from deeper policy failures that have widened spatial schisms within countries. This post reflects on an article, “The Urban-Rural Gap and the Dilemma of Governance” by Anirudh Krishna, on that issue.
item.content:encoded:
Neglect of the rural population is frequently attributed to globalisation. But treating globalisation as the sole culprit averts attention from deeper policy failures that have widened spatial schisms within countries.

This post reflects on an article, “The Urban-Rural Gap and the Dilemma of Governance” by Anirudh Krishna, on that issue.

Although the article was published over a decade ago, its arguments are strikingly relevant today.

In the article, Krishna argues that the divide in today’s world is no longer only between countries but increasingly within countries.

Wealth, opportunity and political influence are concentrated through the process of urban agglomeration and the creation of urban elites while rural areas stagnate in the background.

Fast-growing economies such as China, India, Indonesia and South Africa now contain both those who are elite and those who are poor by global standards.

Krishna shows that poverty rates are consistently higher in rural areas.

For instance, in Malawi, 57 per cent of the rural population lives below the poverty line, compared with 17 per cent of urban residents.

In Zambia, the figures are 78 per cent rural versus 28 per cent urban; in Vietnam, it’s 22 per cent versus five per cent in rural and urban areas, respectively.

Over the decade these numbers might have changed, but still the argument made then holds equally for Papua New Guinea and its Melanesian neighbours, as World Bank data shows (Figure 1).

In Ethiopia, 97 per cent of urban residents have access to improved drinking water, compared to only 42 per cent of rural residents.

In India, 60 per cent of city residents have improved sanitation facilities versus only 25 per cent in rural areas.

In PNG, rural sanitation is at 15 per cent whereas urban sanitation rates hover at 49 per cent.

 Educational attainment is similarly unequal.

In Pakistan, 61 per cent of urban residents complete secondary education, compared with half of that figure for rural residents.

Even when years of schooling are similar, learning outcomes are poorer in rural schools.

Rural areas are poorer, but urban areas are more productive.

According to Krishna, this results in a governance dilemma: should governments prioritise poor rural households or globally competitive households in capital cities?

In theory, democratic systems should balance both.

Despite making up only a small proportion of the total population, urban elites’ preferences shape policy priorities, public investment decisions and national development narratives. 

Governments build airports, information superhighways and elite universities in urban centres, but rural schools, health clinics, roads and internet access are underfunded.

All this results, Krishna argues, in rural populations’ being locked into informal, insecure and sometimes sordid forms of work.

When economic mobility out of rural poverty is blocked, control of the state becomes the most reliable route to wealth.

I can’t help but think of my own country.

During the first general election in 1977, two years after independence, under 900 persons contested in PNG’s national elections.

The last general election in 2022 saw around 3,619 candidates contest the national election.

Even after accounting for population growth, competition for political power has intensified.

There were 31 contestants per 100,000 persons in 1977.

That number rose to 35.5 contestants per 100,000 persons in 2022 – an increase of about 15 per cent. 

Ask any Papua New Guinean about their future aspirations and chances are high they will say that they want to become a politician.

item.title: Chasm between anti-corruption pledges and practicalities
item.description: THE complexities and contradictions of implementing anti-corruption initiatives in a Pacific Island context were on full display in Suva this week. Fiji’s principal anti-corruption body, the Fiji Independent Commission Against Corruption (Ficac), laid charges against Fijian and British national Charlie Charters, alleging two counts of aiding and abetting a whistleblower and the unlawful publicatio
item.content:encoded:
THE complexities and contradictions of implementing anti-corruption initiatives in a Pacific Island context were on full display in Suva this week.

Fiji’s principal anti-corruption body, the Fiji Independent Commission Against Corruption (Ficac), laid charges against Fijian and British national Charlie Charters, alleging two counts of aiding and abetting a whistleblower and the unlawful publication of official Commission information on Facebook between Nov 2 and Dec 14 last year.

Notably, this development follows Ficac’s last January recommendation to a parliamentary standing committee that a Whistleblower Protection Act be established, emphasising the need to shield informants, including civil servants, from retaliation.      

Former journalist Charters was remanded in custody over the weekend before appearing in court on Monday (Feb 23), where he was granted bail.

According to media reports, he said he declined an offer that would have required him to reveal the identity of a whistleblower in exchange for being able to proceed with a planned flight to Sydney.

Charters had previously reported on alleged improper appointments and malpractice within Ficac, which are accusations that remain contested.

The Charters’ case coincides with two significant anti-corruption milestones this month: a sixth anniversary event for the Teieniwa Vision in Suva and the release of Transparency International’s 2025 Corruption Perceptions Index (CPI).

The CPI assesses 182 countries and territories and is widely regarded as a key global measure of perceived public sector corruption.

And the Teieniwa Vision, adopted by Pacific leaders in 2020, explicitly supports whistleblowers, the right to information and the role of independent civil society and responsible media in anti-corruption efforts. The charges against Charters have prompted discussion about the persistent gap in the Pacific between anti-corruption commitments and their practical delivery.

Taken together, the CPI report and the Teieniwa Vision anniversary call for reflection on the state of corruption in the Pacific and the effectiveness of current anti-corruption efforts.

At the anniversary event in Suva, co-hosted by the Pacific Islands Forum (PIF) and the United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime (UNODC), Kiribati President Taneti Maamau, in a message delivered on his behalf, recalled that the Vision arose from a “profound realisation” that corruption was “a rising tide threatening the very foundations of our sovereign nations”.

Such warnings by Maamau and others are arguably more urgent today than ever before.

In addition to historic, pre-existing challenges, the Pacific now faces the growing danger of transnational crime networks and drug cartels, which thrive in regions with weak institutions and enforcement gaps.

This concern was raised during the high-level panel discussion marking the anniversary.

The PIF policy adviser for regional security, Neumi Vakadewabuka, identified governance weaknesses as a key factor driving the expanding presence of organised and transnational crime syndicates in the region.

He noted recent regional surveys indicating that the integrity of immigration and customs systems, law enforcement agencies and broader governance structures across the Pacific is being significantly tested by these syndicates.

Vakadewabuka’s observations are reinforced by a series of major drug seizures across the region.

Last month, Tahitian authorities intercepted a vessel carrying 96 bales of cocaine.

On the same day, Fijian police seized more than 100 bags of suspected cocaine in Tavua, in the Western Division.

The head of the UNODC Pacific office, Marie Pegie Cauchois, agreed on the major threats posed by organised crime.

“The UNODC continues to highlight that corruption creates the openings organised crime needs, while integrity is what closes those doors,” said Cauchois, also a panellist at the discussion.

“The strengthening of transparent institutions, border governance and oversight frameworks is therefore central to safeguarding the region,” she added.

Against this backdrop of rising organised crime, the latest CPI findings take on a grimmer significance.

 The report notes that corruption remains a “serious threat in every part of the world” and that any signs of progress are “limited”.

However, data indicate that corruption is especially pervasive in developing regions such as the Pacific, where, as the Teieniwa Vision underscores, the impact is felt most acutely among vulnerable groups.

The CPI scores countries on a scale of 0 (highly corrupt) to 100 (very clean).

The lowest-scoring Pacific Island country was Papua New Guinea at 26 points – an alarming 19 points below the Asia-Pacific average.

Vanuatu scored 47, Solomon Islands 44 and Fiji 55, indicating little movement in recent years.

While the Teieniwa Vision’s anti-corruption strategy reflects the right intent – stronger practices, adoption of UN frameworks and adherence to the rule of law – the challenge lies in sustained commitment and implementation.

Despite the Vision’s support for the right to information and protection for genuine whistleblowers, hardly any Pacific Island countries have enacted such legislation.

item.title: Sri Lankan Panditha receives award for services to PNG
item.description: UNIVERSITY of Papua New Guinea lecturer Panditha Bandara, from Sri Lanka, and now a permanent resident of PNG, received the Independence King’s Medal this month for his 24 years of service to the country. “I came to PNG in February, 2002. From that day and onwards, I (have been) serving, and available for services. So this medal coincides with that.”
item.content:encoded:
UNIVERSITY of Papua New Guinea lecturer Panditha Bandara, from Sri Lanka, and now a permanent resident of PNG, received the Independence King’s Medal this month for his 24 years of service to the country.

“I came to PNG in February, 2002. From that day and onwards, I (have been) serving, and available for services. So this medal coincides with that.”

Panditha, 76, arrived in PNG in 2002 with his wife, Padma. They have a daughter.

He joined the Goroka Technical College in 2002, as a senior lecturer and deputy principal. He started the Diploma in Accounting programme there.

He was invited to join UPNG in 2007. Now he is a senior lecturer and head of the accounting division at the University of PNG School of Business and Public Policy.

He is also the Honorary Consul for Sri Lanka in PNG.

One of the things he cherishes is the annual Christmas donation to the Cheshire home in December.

“For the past two years, we’ve donated nearly K30,000 worth of goods, glossaries and even cash.”

As Sri Lanka’s Honorary Consul, he has been doing his utmost to strengthen bilateral relationship with PNG.

“The most recent (move) was last week when the Somare Institute of Leadership and Governance and National Institute of Business Management in Sri Lanka signed a technical corporation agreement.”

Panditha had also organised to bring human eye corneas to PNG every month and donate them to the Port Moresby General Hospital.

“These live eye corneas are transplanted to the people who have vision impairment.”

His services are not confined to the university and teaching.

“I go beyond that. I have also been a council member of the Certified Practicing Accounts PNG for nine consecutive years. I’m still serving the accounting profession.”

In addition, he authored 11 books in Business Studies, Accounting and Economics for PNG students “from Grades 9 to 12 and university level”.

He was not expecting any recognition for his services.

“I did not think about any recognition in this manner. I really worked for my pleasure and I don’t expect any medals or any recognition like this, but it came.

“In fact, in 2017, I was recognised for Logohu. Now, I am receiving this 50 th anniversary King’s Independence Medal.”

He thanks his wife Padma for supporting him.

I did not think about any recognition in this manner. I really worked for my pleasure and I don’t expect any medals or any recognition like this, but it came.”

“We’ve been married for 47 years. Her support is remarkable. Without it, I cannot go beyond my office work and extra work. I’m grateful to her.”

“He is always committed to service in his life. It has been recognised, so it will give him the boost to do more. When he needs to do something, I provide everything for him.  When he is doing his work, I never call him to say we go there and there.” 

Panditha had experienced living in different cultural environments before coming to PNG.

“Before I came, some people were talking about PNG this and that. But I said, I am a risk-taker. I have international experience in living in different cultures. PNG is another culture. So it is not a culture shock.”

One thing he has noticed since arriving in PNG is how the country “has changed for the better”.

“When we came here, we didn’t see these supermarkets, buildings and roads and all these things. Now you can see them. It is more or less close to the developed suburb of a developed country.

“Also the (higher) education level and professionalism of young people. These things I have noted and I have contributed to as well. I have directly or indirectly taught between 10,000 and 15,000 young people.”

He encourages young Papua New Guineans to be committed to their goals.

“My advice is: Commit yourself to achieve your goal. You must have a goal. Commit yourself to achieve it.”

He thanks the Government for recognising his services since he arrived in 2002.
